title: update_record opcode — record update syntax
purpose: The `update_record` opcode (opcode 181) is emitted by the OTP 26+ compiler for record update syntax (`Record#record_name{field = NewValue}`). It copies a tuple and updates specific elements in the copy, avoiding the need for a full tuple deconstruction and reconstruction. Without it, any Erlang code using record updates (which is most OTP code) hits `UnsupportedOpcode(181)`. Gleam compiles record updates to this opcode as well.
task: Wire the existing `Instruction::UpdateRecord` variant (already decoded by the loader with a dedicated variant) into the opcode dispatch table and implement the handler. The handler allocates a new tuple on the heap, copies all elements from the source, then overwrites the specified elements with new values.

requirements.title: Opcode dispatch wiring
requirements.spec: Add `Instruction::UpdateRecord` to the dispatch table in `opcodes/mod.rs`, routing to a handler.
acceptance: `Instruction::UpdateRecord { operands }` is matched in the dispatch table
No `UnsupportedOpcode(181)` error when executing modules containing `update_record`

requirements.title: Operand parsing
requirements.spec: The `update_record` operand list has a variable-length encoding. Parse the operands into: hint (atom), arity (unsigned), source (register), destination (register), followed by pairs of (index, value) for each update.
acceptance: Operand list is parsed into structured fields: hint, arity, source, dest, update_pairs
Each update pair contains: element index (unsigned) and new value (register/literal)
The number of update pairs is derived from (operand_count - 4) / 2
Invalid operand counts raise an error
Test: operand list for `update_record(copy, 3, X0, X1, 1, X2)` parses as: source=X0, dest=X1, updates=[(index 1, value X2)]

requirements.title: update_record handler implementation
requirements.spec: Implement the handler. Allocate a new tuple on the heap with the same arity as the source, copy all elements from the source tuple, then overwrite the specified elements with the update values.
acceptance: Source term must be a tuple of the declared arity (badarg otherwise)
A new tuple is allocated on the process heap via `process.heap_mut().alloc()`
All elements from the source are copied to the new tuple
Update pairs overwrite specific elements in the new tuple (1-based indexing matching BEAM convention)
The new tuple term is written to the destination register
Test: `update_record({a, b, c}, dest, [(1, x), (3, z)])` produces `{x, b, z}` in dest
Test: update with no pairs produces an identical copy

requirements.title: Heap allocation and GC safety
requirements.spec: The new tuple allocation must be GC-safe. If the heap is full, GC should be triggered before allocation.
acceptance: Heap allocation follows the same pattern as `put_tuple2` (test_heap or alloc with GC fallback)
Source tuple elements survive GC if collection is triggered mid-allocation
Test: update_record after heavy allocation verifies correct result after potential GC

boundaries: SHALL NOT modify the tuple heap representation — use existing `write_tuple()` and `Tuple` accessors
SHALL NOT implement in-place mutation — record update always creates a new tuple (immutable data)
SHALL NOT implement record field access by name — the compiler resolves names to indices
Note: core.rs is at 583 lines (over 500-line guideline). Consider placing handler in core.rs alongside put_tuple2/get_tuple_element (same semantic family) — adds ~40 lines.
verification: cargo test -p beamr passes with all tests green
